    AI Hot Topics

    1. Google: Launches Tool to Import AI Chat History from Other AI Providers into Gemini

    April 29th, Google (GOOG.O): Launched a tool to import AI chat history from other AI providers into Gemini. At the same time, Gemini launched new personalization features in the UK.

    2. DeepSeek Launches Image Recognition Mode

    April 29th, DeepSeek launched its image recognition mode today, currently in beta testing. This mode may be available in beta testing after updates to the DeepSeek web version and app.

    April 29th, DeepSeek launched its image recognition mode today, currently in beta testing.

    ...

    3. China Merchants Securities International: Microsoft and OpenAI Update Cooperation Agreement, Amazon and Google Benefit

    On April 29, China Merchants Securities International issued a report stating that OpenAI, Microsoft, and Amazon have restructured their cooperation relationship. Microsoft terminated its exclusive right to use OpenAI models and adjusted the cooperation relationship to a non-exclusive agreement, valid until 2032. The cooperation model has also changed from the previous indefinite cooperation linked to AGI to a fixed-term cooperation. Overall, this adjustment marks a shift in the AI ​​industry from a "cloud platform lock-in" to a more open multi-cloud model, strengthening competition between cloud vendors and large models, while also providing enterprise customers with greater flexibility in model and infrastructure combinations. Marginally, the bank believes this is more beneficial to Amazon and Google's cloud businesses, enabling them to access OpenAI models and consolidate their multi-model, multi-partner neutral AI infrastructure strategy. Regarding Microsoft, the bank holds a more neutral and balanced view on this transaction.

    ... The negative factor is that losing the exclusive partnership with OpenAI weakens Azure's differentiated advantage in the AI ​​field, potentially dragging down the marginal growth elasticity of the cloud business. Positive factors include better financial terms, continued large-scale consumption of Azure resources, and the ability to retain approximately 27% of OpenAI's equity and participate in its long-term growth. Furthermore, this arrangement opens up more potential financing channels for OpenAI, to some extent guaranteeing the existing investment value of Microsoft in OpenAI.

    4. OpenAI Refutes Reports of Underperformance, Says Business is "Full Throttle"

    On April 29th, local time, OpenAI refuted external doubts about its sales growth, stating that both its consumer and enterprise businesses are "full throttle." The Wall Street Journal previously cited sources indicating that OpenAI failed to meet several targets, and CFO Sarah Friar had expressed concern that the company might be unable to meet future computing power demands if sales growth was not fast enough.

    ... Following this report, the stock prices of several OpenAI investors and partners, including SoftBank Group, Oracle, and CoreWeave, fell. In an emailed statement, OpenAI said that demand from enterprise clients and its nascent advertising business continued to grow, creating a "very positive internal atmosphere." OpenAI called the Wall Street Journal report "typical sensationalism." The statement said the company still considers expanding computing power a "key driver" to enable it to "provide a better product experience for its customers."

    5. Musk places AI security at the heart of his lawsuit against OpenAI

    On April 29, Elon Musk testified before a federal jury that artificial intelligence "could kill us all," placing AI security at the heart of his lawsuit against OpenAI. In the witness stand, Musk likened the risks of uncontrolled AI to the Terminator, stating that the goal should be a future closer to Star Trek. He argued that OpenAI was originally created to prevent powerful AI from being controlled by profit-driven tech giants.

    ... Musk said these concerns intensified after a 2015 conversation with Google co-founder Larry Page, who allegedly accused him of being a "speciesist" for prioritizing humanity over future digital intelligence. The case revolves around Musk's allegations that OpenAI abandoned its nonprofit mission after ChatGPT helped the company become one of the world's most valuable AI companies. Musk is seeking more than $150 billion in damages from OpenAI and Microsoft. OpenAI's lawyers say Musk lost a leadership battle, left the company, and filed the lawsuit after launching his own rival AI company, xAI. Musk is expected to return to court today for cross-examination.

    DeFi Hot Topics

    1. Visa Integrates Polygon into Global Stablecoin Settlement Program, Annualized Settlement Scale Reaches $7 Billion

    On April 29th, Visa announced the formal integration of Polygon into its global stablecoin settlement program. Visa partners, including card issuers and acquirers, can now complete stablecoin settlements through the Polygon network. Visa stated that its stablecoin settlement program's annualized operating scale reached $7 billion this quarter, a 50% increase compared to three months ago. Polygon emphasizes that its network features tiered transaction fees (Asia and America), approximately 4-second transaction final confirmation time, and has been used by institutions such as Stripe, Revolut, Flutterwave, and BlackRock for real financial activity settlements.

    4. Lido DAO Proposal to Temporarily Lower EarnETH Vault First Loss Protection Threshold to Cover Kelp-Related Losses

    On April 29th, a new proposal was launched on the Lido Research Forum, suggesting that the DAO authorize a temporary reduction in the first loss protection trigger threshold for the EarnETH vault during the Kelp event.

    Under current rules, Lido Earn's protection mechanism only triggers when vault losses exceed 1% of holdings. However, the proposal points out that if the rsETH shortage is resolved through DeFi United, the actual loss may be less than 1%. To ensure EarnETH users receive full compensation, the proposal suggests a one-time exception in this case, allowing the use of the first-loss fund to cover losses below the 1% threshold. The estimated residual borrowing interest loss is approximately 400 to 600 ETH. The proposal emphasizes that this is a one-time arrangement for user protection, brand reputation, and business considerations, and does not change the general 1% rule. Given that the rsETH incident is expected to be resolved within 5 to 10 days, while the standard voting window is 7 days, the proposal also has a time urgency.

    5. Solana's RWA ecosystem has reached a record high total value of approximately $2.5 billion

    On April 29th, according to Cointelegraph, Solana's RWA ecosystem has reached a record high total value of approximately $2.5 billion.

data.title: New regulations from the China Securities Regulatory Commission: Retail investors can now trade tokenized products on the secondary market.
data.description:
On April 20, 2026, the Hong Kong Securities and Futures Commission (SFC) issued a circular regarding the trading of tokenized SFC-approved investment products (hereinafter referred to as tokenized products) on the secondary market, providing a new trading channel for these products. This framework primarily facilitates automated on-screen trading by tokenized open-ended funds through the SFC-licensed Virtual Asset Trading Platform (VATP), further expanding regulated trading options for retail investors. The SFC also stated that it will consider allowing off-exchange secondary market trading arrangements on a case-by-case basis. Image source: SFC. As of March 2026, Hong Kong has already offered 13 tokenized products to the public, with the total assets under management of the related shares rising to approximately HK$10.7 billion in the past year. This new arrangement draws on the experience of Hong Kong's mature ETF market and licensed virtual asset platforms, aiming to make trading these tokenized products more convenient and gradually integrate them into the local Web3 ecosystem. How will trading proceed? Simply put, retail investors will be able to buy and sell tokenized products on licensed platforms (such as HashKey and OSL), which will use the existing rules and risk controls for trading virtual assets. Of course, the platform will only execute a trade if your account has sufficient funds or corresponding holdings. Before a product launch, the product provider must work with the platform to test the process, monitor risks, and ensure system stability. This arrangement has one advantage: after traditional securities products are tokenized, they can be traded even in the evenings and on weekends, unlike traditional markets which are limited to weekday trading hours. Combined with regulated stablecoins and subsequent tokenized deposits, this can improve market liquidity and provide more options for responding to rapidly changing markets. To ensure fair pricing, all parties must take responsibility. To guarantee reasonable buying and selling prices, the platform needs to do several things: if the transaction price deviates too much from the product's net asset value (NAV), a warning will be displayed; investors should also be reminded that they can choose to subscribe or redeem at NAV in the primary market, although the timing and mechanism will differ. The platform will also use its existing monitoring system to minimize price fluctuations and manipulation. If you trade through a broker on the platform, you should also see these warnings on the interface. All trading processes and restrictions are aligned as closely as possible with the current requirements of the Hong Kong Stock Exchange, including setting a cooling-off period and trading volatility limits, etc., to protect the basic rights of retail investors. Who will support liquidity? Product providers must strive to find at least one market maker, and if a market maker wants to withdraw, it must give three months' notice. In addition, product providers themselves must closely monitor the buying and selling situation in the secondary market, communicate frequently with market makers, prepare contingency plans, and appoint compliant distributors to help handle subscriptions and redemptions. The platform is responsible for reviewing and monitoring the performance of market makers, ensuring that their bid-ask spreads, maintenance periods, etc., meet the standards. If market makers perform poorly, the platform must urge them to correct their behavior. Both distributors and market makers must abide by the rules; if there are market-making incentives supporting market manipulation activities, they must also comply with anti-market misconduct requirements. In short, the goal is to prevent trading from becoming too sluggish. However, tokenized products are still in their early stages, and the stability of liquidity is a concern, especially during periods of high volatility or extreme situations. It's crucial to clearly explain the risks. For example, trading volume may be low, and the price may significantly premium or discount relative to net asset value, particularly during weekdays outside of Hong Kong trading hours or on weekends. The clearer this is explained, the better investors can determine if the product is suitable for them.

Speculations on Combining with the Stablecoin Ecosystem

Currently, most retail tokenized products are mainly money market funds. Combined with the stablecoin ecosystem, they could potentially enable on-chain real-time interest collection and 24/7 subscription and redemption, supplementing the limitations of stablecoins themselves in terms of interest income. With the implementation of Hong Kong's stablecoin regulatory framework, the combination of tokenized products and regulated stablecoins may provide more liquidity support for on-chain traditional asset trading, forming an important part of the stablecoin ecosystem.

Furthermore, the gradual increase in 24/7 on-chain transaction volume (including categories such as commodities) also reflects the market's demand for all-weather hedging tools. The new guidelines, introduced against this backdrop, may provide investors with additional trading flexibility in the face of geopolitical uncertainties. In summary, these new regulations represent another important attempt by Hong Kong in the field of tokenization. The first batch of products is expected to be mainly tokenized money market funds, and the Securities and Futures Commission will review the operation and consider expanding the scope as appropriate. For investors, it offers an option to trade tokenized products on a regulated platform, improving accessibility and convenience; for the market, it has the potential to promote further integration of traditional assets and the digital ecosystem. However, the secondary market for tokenized products is still in its early stages, and issues such as liquidity and price stability cannot be resolved overnight. It is recommended that everyone carefully review the sales documents, understand the primary market subscription and redemption mechanism, and then decide based on their own risk tolerance before participating.

data.title: Why does Ethereum still emphasize security and decentralization?
data.description: At the Hong Kong Web3 Carnival, Vitalik Buterin, unsurprisingly, brought up the age-old question: What exactly should Ethereum become? For the past few years, the crypto industry has been accustomed to using performance metrics to discuss public chain competition—for example, whoever has higher TPS, faster confirmation speed, lower gas, etc., is more easily understood as the "next-generation infrastructure," etc. However, by 2026, whether it's Ethereum or new public chains, the challenges they face are no longer limited to internal issues like DeFi, NFTs, L2 scaling, and on-chain finance—AI Coding is rapidly absorbing everything, and progress in formal verification and zero-knowledge proofs is also evident. All of this means that the world facing public chains is undergoing a new transformation. So, do we still need a public infrastructure that everyone can verify, exit from, self-manage, and is not controlled by a single point of failure? So how exactly did Ethereum think about and prepare for this? I. What is Ethereum actually used for: Bulletin Board and Computation? "Ethereum is not meant to compete with high-frequency trading platforms, Ethereum is not meant to be the fastest chain, Ethereum aims to be a secure chain, a decentralized chain, a chain that will always be online, a chain you can always rely on," Vitalik explained in this speech, reinterpreting Ethereum's value with two very basic concepts: First, Ethereum is like a "public bulletin board"; second, Ethereum provides "computation" capabilities. These two simple concepts not only summarize the new positioning direction of Ethereum that we have been discussing recently, but also almost encapsulate the fundamental reason why Ethereum differs from ordinary internet services. The so-called public bulletin board is not an abstract metaphor; it means that applications can publish messages on Ethereum, and everyone can see the content and order of these messages—these messages can be transactions, hash values, encrypted data, or other information that needs to be publicly committed to, ordered, and verified. This is also the most fundamental difference between Ethereum and ordinary servers. Servers can be faster, cheaper, and more efficient, but they typically require users to trust that the operators will not tamper with records, refuse service, or shut down the system at critical moments; Ethereum aims to provide this capability. Computation, on the other hand, refers to Ethereum's ability to allow people to create shared digital objects controlled by code. These objects can be ERC-20 tokens, NFTs, ENS names, or even DAOs, on-chain organizations, financial protocols, or other more complex applications. Therefore, **by 2026, if we still understand Ethereum solely in terms of "performance," or compare new public chains only using TPS, gas costs, and confirmation speeds, we'll easily miss the real problems Ethereum needs to solve.** The focus of public chain scaling in the coming years will no longer be just "making the chain faster," but rather how to maintain verifiability, decentralization, and user self-sovereignty in more complex application scenarios. In other words, scaling isn't about transforming Ethereum into another centralized, high-performance system, but about enabling more applications to run without sacrificing the underlying trust assumptions.** This is also a key reason why Vitalik has reconsidered L2 since the beginning of this year. In short, he believes the market has historically viewed L2 as a scaling tool for Ethereum, migrating more transactions to L2 when the mainnet becomes expensive or congested. However, L2's historical mission is now complete, and it shouldn't just remain at the level of "transaction diversion," but rather become the forefront for Ethereum's expansion into more application scenarios. This judgment is particularly important for the Ethereum ecosystem today. In the past few years, the market has often simplified L2 to "cheaper Ethereum." But in Vitalik's framework, L2 is not a simple replacement layer, but a functional extension built around Ethereum's public infrastructure layer. L1 undertakes the most critical roles of commitment, settlement, data publication, and verification, while L2 and off-chain systems provide higher-frequency, more flexible, and more privacy-friendly execution capabilities around specific application scenarios. This is why Vitalik is unwilling to make "fastest" Ethereum's primary goal. Speed ​​is certainly important, but if the cost of speed is that ordinary users cannot run nodes, verify states, or protect themselves when the system malfunctions, then the chain will gradually become a less efficient centralized service. For Ethereum, speed is merely a matter of user experience; security and decentralization are the reasons for its existence. II. In the AI ​​Era, Ethereum's Value Will Be Amplified Again The most noteworthy point of this speech is that Vitalik did not treat AI merely as an external hot topic, but rather placed it within the technical context of Ethereum's future roadmap. For example, the Ethereum community has already begun to experiment with using AI to generate code proofs to demonstrate that the software version running Ethereum possesses the characteristics it should have. Two years ago, this would have been difficult, but the rapid development of AI is making software security verification much easier. This corresponds to a very real problem: as the assets, identities, organizations, and rules carried by the blockchain increase, the cost of code vulnerabilities also rises. If AI can help developers discover vulnerabilities, generate proofs, and assist in formal verification, then it will not only be an efficiency tool at the application layer but also become part of protocol security engineering. However, the deeper impact of AI on Ethereum is not only on the development side but also on the user side, especially changing the way ordinary users interact with digital systems. As we all know, human-computer interaction has undergone several changes over the past few decades. Initially, users interacted with computers through command lines, and only a few technically savvy individuals could truly use complex systems. Later, with the popularization of graphical interfaces and mobile apps, ordinary users could complete operations through buttons, pages, and menus. Now, AI is pushing interaction methods towards natural language. Users no longer need to understand every step of the operation; they only need to state their goal, and the system may automatically break down the path, call tools, and complete the execution. This change will have an even greater impact on Web3. Today, for a user to complete a cross-chain DeFi operation, they often need to choose a network, confirm the gas, authorize the contract, execute the swap, bridge assets, and then deposit into the protocol. Each step requires a signature, and each step is prone to errors. In the future, if AI agents become an important entry point for wallets and on-chain applications, users may only need to say, "Exchange some of my ETH for stablecoins and deposit them into the yield protocol according to a low-risk strategy." The remaining path planning, protocol selection, transaction simulation, and execution may all be handled by intelligent agents. This sounds like it would greatly lower the barrier to entry, but problems arise as well. When users no longer manually click through each step, and when an AI agent understands user intent, invokes contracts, and initiates transactions on their behalf, how can users confirm that it hasn't overstepped its authority? How can they know that the path it chooses isn't malicious? How can the capabilities of reversal, verification, and self-protection be preserved without sacrificing the user experience? This is precisely where Ethereum's value is being amplified again. AI can make operations more natural, but natural language itself doesn't bring trust. A smarter interface, if still backed by an unverifiable black box system, means that users have simply shifted from "trusting the platform" to "trusting the model." Ethereum, on the other hand, provides more like a trust infrastructure for the AI ​​era. Extending this further, it also makes the role of wallets more important. Therefore, future wallets may no longer be just "signature tools" or "asset lists," but will gradually become the permission management layer between users and AI agents, on-chain applications, identity systems, and payment networks. This is because users need to set boundaries through their wallets, such as which operations can be executed automatically, which operations require secondary confirmation, which assets cannot be accessed, and which authorizations need to be checked and cleaned up regularly. Interestingly, just before Vitalik reinterpreted Ethereum from the perspective of the protocol roadmap, the Ethereum Foundation also released the EF Mandate, which can be considered a formal confirmation of this roadmap at the value level. This Mandate document states that the ultimate reason for Ethereum's existence is to protect users' self-sovereignty—users can independently control their own assets, identity, behavior, and choices, without relying on any centralized intermediaries. Regarding this point, the EF Mandate proposed an acronym "CROPS," an acronym for Censorship Resistance, Open Source, Privacy, and Security. In the foundation's statement, Ethereum must first maintain these four attributes; without them, Ethereum loses its reason for being used, built, and protected. Objectively speaking, the terms censorship resistance, open source, privacy, and security are not new in the Web3 context; they have been repeatedly discussed since the very beginning of the crypto industry. However, the re-emphasis on them today carries a significantly different meaning. After all, early discussions of these values ​​in the crypto industry were largely aimed at opposing centralized platforms and financial intermediaries. Now, these values ​​face new challenges in the AI ​​era. EF Mandate points out that **future centralization may not necessarily manifest as a platform forcibly controlling you; it could also manifest as you having no idea how the system (especially AI) makes decisions for you.** For example, when recommendation algorithms determine what you see, when AI assistants filter information for you, when smart agents execute transactions for you, and when identity, assets, and data are all encapsulated in interfaces, user sovereignty may be implicitly diluted with each "more convenient" experience. Therefore, **Ethereum's decentralization cannot be understood merely as the number of nodes, client diversity, or consensus mechanisms. It should be understood as a decentralized system where no single entity can easily change the rules, where users can verify the system state, developers can build freely, applications can be publicly audited, and assets and identity do not have to be completely entrusted to the platform.** Vitalik also emphasized that decentralization is not a feature of Ethereum, but rather the reason for its existence. Without decentralization, Ethereum would simply be a less efficient centralized service. This statement also explains why the Ethereum community consistently emphasizes that "everyone is a Builder." In the Web2 era, most users are merely consumers of the product. Platforms define rules, and users accept those rules; platforms change their interfaces, and users adapt to them; platforms shut down services, and users can only migrate or abandon them. However, in the Ethereum ecosystem, builders are not just a small group of core developers, but also wallet developers, DApp developers, node operators, researchers, educators, auditors, community contributors, and even every ordinary user who diligently manages their private keys, learns about on-chain security, and participates in governance discussions. This means that CROPS should not remain just a slogan; it needs to be implemented through concrete products and actions. Taking a wallet like imToken as an example, security is not just a prompt, but a complete experience encompassing mnemonic phrase management, risk alerts, DApp authorization management, transaction parsing, and phishing detection. In conclusion, returning to Vitalik's speech at the Hong Kong Web3 Carnival, it superficially outlined the technology roadmap for the next five years: scaling, zkEVM, post-quantum security, formal verification, privacy, block building, account abstraction, zkVM, and so on. But at a deeper level, it was actually answering a value question: When the entire industry is pursuing faster and cheaper performance, what should Ethereum optimize for? The answer isn't to sacrifice performance or user experience, but rather to make on-chain applications more accessible to ordinary users, and this must serve self-sovereignty, security, verifiability, and fair participation, rather than trading these for short-term efficiency. The AI ​​era will make this question even more acute. From this perspective, what's truly important for Ethereum in the next phase may not be whether it can become the fastest chain, but whether it can continue to be the most trustworthy, most verifiable, and least reliant on a single point of authority in public infrastructure.

data.title: Tonight, Powell's "last dance"
data.description:
The outcome of the Fed's April FOMC meeting is virtually a foregone conclusion—interest rates will remain unchanged. However, the real focus of this meeting lies in the signals that Powell, in his final policy meeting as chairman, will send, and whether the committee will formally convey a hawkish stance that "rate cuts are essentially out of the question." The Fed will announce its interest rate decision at 2:00 AM Beijing time on April 30th. The benchmark interest rate is expected to remain unchanged in the 3.5% to 3.75% range, with a high degree of market consensus. Only Governor Miran is expected to dissent, supporting a 25 basis point rate cut. The latest developments stem from the inflation front. The Iran war and energy shocks continue to disrupt the outlook, with gasoline prices remaining above $4 and traffic in the Strait of Hormuz still heavily hampered. Meanwhile, recent employment data has shown resilience, reducing the urgency among dovish committee members to quickly support the labor market. Federal Reserve officials generally expect the decline in inflation to be delayed by another full year. Market expectations for rate cuts have narrowed significantly; Deutsche Bank has withdrawn its previous forecast of a September rate cut, adjusting its baseline scenario to the Fed "holding rates steady indefinitely" near the neutral rate. The core debate at this meeting focused on the wording of the statement and the risk assessment at the press conference—the addition or deletion of a single word in the forward guidance could send drastically different policy signals to the market. Meanwhile, with the U.S. Department of Justice ending its investigation into Powell, Kevin Warsh's path to nomination as Federal Reserve Chairman is essentially clear, making this meeting even more historically significant. The consensus is to hold rates steady, with the debate shifting to the "next step." This FOMC meeting did not include a dot plot, and interest rates themselves were virtually a foregone conclusion. The focus is whether the Fed will continue to hint at a "more likely next step is a rate cut" policy, or begin to acknowledge that the risks have shifted in both directions. According to Bank of America, the current inflation outlook is as unclear as it was at the March meeting. While stock market trading suggests the Iran war is over, energy and shipping disruptions persist, and the transmission of the conflict to core inflation remains highly uncertain. The employment side did not provide sufficient reason for the Fed to rush into a dovish stance. March non-farm payrolls, ADP, and initial jobless claims data all showed resilience in the labor market, even exhibiting some signs of improvement. This means that members who previously advocated for rate cuts are finding it more difficult to continue emphasizing "downside risks to employment" as a primary policy basis. The dovish stance has also begun to tighten, reducing the urgency for rate cuts. Prior to this meeting, the most noticeable change within the Fed was the tightening of statements by previously dovish members. Waller's speech last week not only emphasized the upside risks to inflation from the Iran war, but also mentioned the labor supply shock. He believes this means the economy may need "little or no net job creation" to maintain a stable unemployment rate. Bank of America believes Waller may still want to cut interest rates this year, but the magnitude and timing may be less than previously expected. Daly went a step further. She stated that if policy remains unchanged throughout the year, this will provide a good constraint on inflation without harming the labor market. She also believes the impact of the Iran war on inflation may be greater than its impact on growth, and Daly's current baseline scenario has shifted to a flat interest rate path throughout the year. Even Miran, the most dovish member of the FOMC, indicated he prefers three rate cuts this year rather than four, citing a worsening inflation mix since the beginning of the year. Bank of America believes that if the April meeting shows a dot plot, some members' 2026 interest rate expectations have already shifted upwards, and by June, the risk of further upward shifts is increasing.

Statement wording: A single word difference, vastly different signals

The biggest highlight of this FOMC statement is whether the Federal Reserve will hint that the policy path risk has shifted to "two-way".

The biggest highlight of this FOMC statement is whether the Federal Reserve will hint that the policy path risk has shifted to "two-way".

The current statement's use of the phrase "additional adjustments" implicitly suggests a dovish assumption that the next step will be a rate cut. Changing it to "any adjustments" or removing "additional" would mean the next step would no longer be presupposed as a rate cut, and the policy path would officially shift towards two-way openness. The March meeting minutes show that the number of committee members supporting the adoption of a two-way risk statement increased from "several" in January to "some," and the wording became more firm. Bank of America believes this is a close 50/50 assessment, but most members still prefer to maintain the existing forward guidance language. Deutsche Bank, however, tends to believe that substantive guidance adjustments will be postponed until June, when the committee will have a clearer understanding of the Middle East situation, labor market stability, and the inflation transmission path, but the risks are clearly skewed towards a hawkish stance. In addition, the statement is expected to include an adjustment: given the downward revision of fourth-quarter GDP and weak consumer spending in January and February, the Fed may downgrade its description of economic activity from "solid" to "moderate." However, Bank of America points out that this adjustment itself has a dovish tone, which contradicts the committee's overall intention to send a hawkish signal to the market. Press Conference: Powell's Tough Stance is Inevitable If this is indeed Powell's last press conference as chairman, he will most likely maintain a moderately hawkish stance. According to Bank of America, Powell's core message is likely that the Fed will firmly hold rates steady, and current policy is well-prepared to address the risks of its dual mandate. Given the continued high level of uncertainty, the Fed has no reason to contradict the market's pricing in a flat interest rate path. The most sensitive issue at the press conference is the threshold for raising interest rates. If Powell reiterates that a rate hike is not the baseline scenario for a majority on the committee, the market may interpret this as a dovish signal. If he emphasizes the importance of completing the anti-inflation mission, or points out that inflation has been above target for several consecutive years, it will be seen as a hawkish signal. It's worth noting that "inflation" was mentioned 67 times at the March press conference, while "labor market/employment/unemployment" was mentioned only 40 times, clearly indicating that inflation has become the heaviest weight on the policy scale. It's expected that he won't provide a quantitative threshold for interest rate hikes. Regarding the Iran war, Powell is expected to acknowledge both upside risks to inflation and downside risks to growth and the labor market. However, the market is more focused on which side he leans towards. If his stance is close to Daly's, meaning the war's impact on inflation is greater than its impact on growth, the market may perceive him as very hawkish. The focus is on whether interest rate cuts will be shelved or merely postponed. Nick Timiraos, often referred to as the "new Fed mouthpiece," wrote in a pre-meeting article that the April meeting marks a turning point in a deeper policy debate: how long can the Fed maintain its stance that the next step is more likely to be a rate cut than a rate hike? Timiraos pointed out that two years ago, Powell downplayed concerns about stagflation, saying, "I see neither stagflation nor inflation." But now, the combined effects of the war-induced energy shock and inflation that has yet to return to the 2% target make the historical mirror of the stagflation of the 1970s seem less distant than it once was. He emphasized that the Federal Reserve is observing how the US economy digests its fourth supply shock in five years, including the resurgence of the pandemic, the Russia-Ukraine conflict, the tariff dispute, and the Iran war. Each shock, viewed individually, might be interpreted as an isolated event requiring no policy response, but their cumulative effect makes managing inflation expectations more challenging. Timiraos believes the statement itself may be as important as the interest rate decision. If the Fed modifies the wording of its formal statement, implying that a rate cut is essentially out of the question, its market impact could be no less significant than a policy action. The final dance and the handover of power. This meeting is attracting even more attention because it may be Powell's last FOMC meeting as chairman. Powell's term as Federal Reserve chairman expires on May 15, and he has pledged to serve as interim chairman until a successor is confirmed. With the DOJ ceasing its investigation into matters related to Powell, Kevin Warsh's path to Senate confirmation is clearer. UBS expects Kevin Warsh to be sworn in before the June 16-17 FOMC meeting. If this happens, the April meeting will be the last full policy communication window of the Powell era, and the market will pay more attention to whether it leaves a policy starting point of "no rate cuts for a longer period" for the next chairman. Market Reaction: Tail Risks Beyond the Event Background Goldman Sachs' trading desk view indicates that the market as a whole regards this FOMC meeting as a low-volatility event, but different assets still have directional sensitivities. Regarding interest rates, Goldman Sachs analyst Brian Bingham expects the statement to lack a significant shift in hawkish inflation rhetoric, with Powell reiterating a wait-and-see approach. However, with only about 5 basis points priced in by December, the threshold for further significant selling and factoring in a substantial rate hike is high. If the baseline scenario deviates, the risks are more likely to point to higher interest rates, fewer rate cuts, and a flatter yield curve. Regarding foreign exchange, Goldman Sachs trader Carlie Ladda believes a slightly hawkish stance from the Fed might generate some dollar buying, but is unlikely to create a sustained rally. The market remains more focused on the situation in Iran, corporate earnings reports, and month-end factors. Trading desks tend to sell dollars when they rebound. Regarding stocks, Goldman Sachs' Vickie Chang points out that the main risk the FOMC poses to the stock market lies in Powell's potentially more cautious emphasis on the inflationary risks from commodity price shocks, which could dampen risk appetite. Currently, risk assets have largely downplayed the impact of the conflict, and downside tail risks may be underestimated.

data.title: The UAE's exit from OPEC marks a derailment for a major oil-producing nation.
data.description:
On April 28, 2026, the United Arab Emirates (UAE) announced through its national news agency WAM that it would formally withdraw from the Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries (OPEC) and its extended alliance, OPEC+, effective May 1. This member, which had been a member for nearly 60 years, produced approximately 3.6 million barrels per day, accounting for about 12% of OPEC's total production, making it the third-largest oil producer after Saudi Arabia and Iraq. After its withdrawal, OPEC's member countries will be reduced from 12 to 11, and the organization's share of global crude oil supply will further decline from approximately 30% to around 26%. This is the largest member withdrawal event OPEC has experienced in recent years.

From Founding to Core: 60 Years of the UAE

OPEC was initially initiated in 1960 by five countries: Iran, Iraq, Kuwait, Saudi Arabia, and Venezuela. Its core purpose was to coordinate production and defend the common interests of oil-exporting countries.

In 1967, the Emirate of Abu Dhabi joined as an independent nation, and four years later, the United Arab Emirates inherited this membership upon its independence.

In the decades that followed, relying on the large-scale capital investment of the Abu Dhabi National Oil Company, the UAE has expanded its energy footprint significantly. Its proven reserves now reach 113 billion barrels, ranking sixth globally and accounting for approximately 6% of global reserves.

Entering the 2020s, the UAE's daily crude oil production stabilized at around 3.6 million barrels, reaching a historical high of nearly 4.12 million barrels in 2022. Meanwhile, Abu Dhabi National Oil Company continued its expansion plan, aiming to increase capacity to 5 million barrels per day by 2027, with a cumulative investment exceeding US$150 billion. While production capacity is increasing, the amount and method of sales are not entirely within the UAE's control. The long-term tension between quotas and capacity: OPEC's core operating mechanism is the quota system. Based on member countries' capacity, historical output, and market forecasts, each member is allocated a production ceiling; exceeding this limit is theoretically a violation. This mechanism can maintain market stability during periods of high oil prices, but for members with rapid capacity expansion, it acts as an invisible revenue ceiling. The UAE is in a similar situation. Its latest quota is approximately 3.41 million barrels per day, while its actual production capacity is close to 4.85 million barrels per day, leaving a gap of approximately 1.4 million to 2 million barrels per day. Based on international oil prices of $70 to $80 per barrel, this suppressed capacity represents a potential annual revenue loss of between $46 billion and $58 billion. The conflict between the UAE and OPEC most culminated in 2021. At that time, demand began to rebound after the COVID-19 pandemic, and OPEC internally discussed whether to continue production cuts. The UAE explicitly refused to accept the existing quota, demanding that the benchmark be increased from 3.2 million barrels to 3.8 million barrels. Negotiations stalled for two weeks, and Saudi Arabia eventually allowed the UAE to raise its quota to 3.65 million barrels. Subsequently, the UAE began to routinely exceed its quota at the implementation level, with daily production exceeding the quota by hundreds of thousands of barrels becoming commonplace in 2024. There was precedent for withdrawal. In OPEC's history, member withdrawals are not uncommon. Indonesia joined in 1962, subsequently withdrew and rejoined, and finally left again in 2016. Ecuador withdrew in 2019. Qatar, after becoming the world's largest exporter of liquefied natural gas, announced its departure in 2019, citing a shift in strategic focus towards natural gas rather than oil. Angola withdrew in 2024, also due to dissatisfaction with its quota allocation. However, the UAE's scale is not comparable to these countries. At the time of its withdrawal, Qatar's daily production was approximately 600,000 barrels, Angola's was approximately 1.1 million barrels, while the UAE's was close to 3.6 million barrels, several times the combined production of all previous withdrawing members. This is because the UAE's economy is more diversified and less reliant on high oil prices to balance its budget than Saudi Arabia's, thus it prefers to compete on volume rather than price. The war disrupted the rhythm, but it wasn't the root cause. On February 28, 2026, the United States and Israel launched a military strike against Iran, and the ensuing conflict quickly spread throughout the Gulf region. The Strait of Hormuz, the world's most important oil shipping route, normally carries about one-fifth of the world's crude oil and liquefied natural gas, but with the escalation of the conflict, the strait was effectively closed. The UAE's exports were almost immediately devastated. Although an overland pipeline bypasses the Strait of Hormuz with a maximum capacity of approximately 1.8 million barrels per day, this is far from enough to compensate for the losses caused by the disruption of maritime transport. In March 2026, its daily crude oil production plummeted to approximately 1.9 million to 2.34 million barrels, a decrease of about 35% to 47% from the pre-war level of 3.6 million barrels. In comparison, Saudi Arabia's production fell by about 23% during the same period, while Iran, as a party to the conflict, saw its production decline by only about 6%. Data from the International Energy Agency shows that OPEC+'s share of global oil production fell from about 48% in February 2026 to 44% in March, and is expected to continue to decline in April, further shrinking in May with the formal withdrawal of the UAE. The disruption of the Strait of Hormuz is a catalyst, but it is only a catalyst. UAE Energy Minister Suhail al-Mazroui explicitly stated that the decision was made after a comprehensive assessment of the UAE's oil production policy and current and future capacity, with policy considerations preceding the current geopolitical conflict. What changes will occur in OPEC's structure? The core indicator for assessing the practical significance of the UAE's withdrawal from OPEC is spare capacity. Spare capacity refers to standby production that can be quickly deployed in a short period, serving as the most important stabilizer for the oil market during supply shocks. Globally, the total effective spare capacity is approximately 4 to 5 million barrels per day, with a significant proportion concentrated in Saudi Arabia and the UAE. After withdrawal, this portion of the UAE's spare capacity will no longer be subject to OPEC quotas and can operate independently of the organization's decision-making system. The UAE is the only OPEC member besides Saudi Arabia with substantial spare capacity. Its withdrawal will reduce OPEC's overall production control, and coupled with continued production increases from non-OPEC oil-producing countries, particularly the US, the room for maneuver in coordinating supply will further shrink. The US currently produces over 13 million barrels per day, higher than Saudi Arabia's approximately 9 million barrels, which has significantly weakened OPEC's bargaining power in recent years. Now, Saudi Arabia will be almost the only OPEC member with large-scale spare capacity, bearing a heavier burden in market management but with fewer available support resources. On the day the withdrawal was announced, how did oil prices react? Brent crude futures initially fell briefly on the day the news was released, but then rose about 2% from the previous day's closing price, with prices above $111 per barrel. The Strait of Hormuz remains effectively blocked, and the UAE cannot substantially increase its exports in the short term. The impact of leaving OPEC on immediate supply is close to zero. Oil prices are still largely driven by geopolitical risks, and are more than 50% higher than pre-war levels in February 2026. However, in the medium to long term, once the strait returns to normal, the expectation of independent increased production from the UAE will put downward pressure on prices. The futures market is relatively cautious in its medium- to long-term reactions. If the UAE achieves its 5 million barrels per day production target and significantly increases production, the new supply will account for approximately 1% to 2% of global demand. This level of increase is sufficient to influence price trends during periods of supply-demand equilibrium. The UAE's Next Step in Production Increase: After exiting the quota system, the UAE can make its own production decisions and will no longer be bound by quotas. The pace and magnitude of the production increase will depend primarily on when the Strait of Hormuz reopens, the progress of Abu Dhabi National Oil Company's capacity construction, and the demand situation in major global consumer markets. Abu Dhabi National Oil Company has been expanding upstream investment over the past few years, and its recoverable capacity is approaching 4.85 million barrels per day. The 5 million barrels per day target for 2027 has already been set. The real significance of exiting the quota system lies in allowing this capacity to be released into the market without restrictions. The UAE also possesses the Habashan Pipeline, connecting inland oil fields to the port of Fujairah, bypassing the Strait of Hormuz to enter the Gulf of Oman, with a maximum daily transport capacity of approximately 1.5-1.8 million barrels. With the Strait not yet fully open to traffic, this pipeline is currently the UAE's limited export route, but it is insufficient to support a full-scale production increase. A World Bank report indicates that the oil supply losses caused by the conflict in Iran are the largest on record, and global energy prices are expected to rise by an average of about a quarter this year. It is estimated that it will take six months for the Strait to recover to pre-war levels. This window of opportunity will be crucial for the UAE to adjust its pace and fully increase production.

data.title: a16z Report: How Stablecoins Are Reshaping the Underlying Logic of Money
data.description:
While those outside the cryptocurrency world may not have realized it, the rebuilding of financial infrastructure has already crossed a critical point.

Stablecoins were once considered a niche trading tool, primarily used for transferring funds between exchanges. But according to a recent report from a16z crypto, this role has undergone a qualitative change.. Stablecoins are becoming a completely new "financial pipeline," upon which a new generation of global financial products is rapidly growing.

This transformation is not only technological but also structural: it is giving rise to a completely new "Banking-as-a-Service" model.

The Divergence of Architectures In the past, people were used to thinking that all blockchains were competing on the same track. But in 2026, infrastructure has differentiated into three distinct types of "tracks" based on performance: General-purpose networks: Such as the public chains commonly found in the market, they remain the mainstay of the crypto capital market (trading, lending, DeFi). Payment-Specific Networks: This is a new category tailored for financial services. Examples include Stripe's Tempo and Circle's Arc. Their optimization focus is no longer on throughput limits, but rather on stablecoin-native gas fees, privacy guarantees, and predictable transaction costs—crucial for fintech companies that need to process millions of orders and must model costs. Institutional-Grade Networks: Such as Canton, designed specifically for regulated entities. They offer programmability and privacy while retaining the legally required compliance framework. This layering means that financial institutions no longer need to make various adjustments to adapt to decentralized networks, but can instead choose the most suitable stack based on their business scenarios. Regulation used to be the biggest obstacle for institutions entering the stablecoin space, but now it is becoming an amplifier of growth. With the passage of the U.S. GENIUS Act, a frantic race has begun among stablecoin issuers to obtain National Trust Licenses from the OCC (Office of the Comptroller of the Currency). This is not merely about gaining legal recognition, but also about securing long-term status within the payment hierarchy. If license holders can directly access the Federal Reserve's clearing system in the future, these issuers will directly occupy a prominent position in the financial system, becoming a new cornerstone of credit and capital markets. Meanwhile, while Europe's MiCA framework led to the forced liquidation of some non-compliant assets (such as USDT), it also unexpectedly spurred a boom in non-USD stablecoins. Data shows that the monthly trading volume of non-USD stablecoins has stabilized between $15 billion and $25 billion. When Stablecoins Become "More Localized" A16z's report reveals several counterintuitive trends indicating that stablecoins are becoming more localized and commercialized: 1. The Explosion of Commercial Payments While C2C still accounts for the majority of transactions (approximately 790 million in 2025), C2B is growing the fastest, with a year-on-year growth of 128%. Monthly deposits in stablecoin card infrastructure surged from almost zero at the end of 2024 to $300 million per month in early 2026. 2. Currency Turnover Rate Doubled. The "speed" of stablecoin circulation, i.e., the ratio of monthly transfer volume to circulating supply, climbed from 2.6 times in early 2024 to 6 times in early 2026. This means that the existing stablecoin supply is no longer a held asset, but rather operates at high speed as a genuine payment network. 3. Unexpected "Localization" While stablecoins have long been touted as a powerful tool for cross-border transactions, data shows that the proportion of cross-border activities is actually declining, while the proportion of domestic transactions has increased from approximately 50% in early 2024 to 75% in early 2026. For example, Brazil's BRLA saw its monthly transaction volume grow from zero to $400 million by connecting to the country's real-time payment system, PIX. For individuals, stablecoins represent economic empowerment—allowing freelancers in Latin America or farmers in Africa to hold and use US dollars without a US bank account, thus combating the devaluation of their local currencies.

Credit, the “Second Act” of the Stablecoin Narrative

If payments are the first act of stablecoins, then credit will be the more disruptive second act.

When trillions of dollars of stablecoin funds are deposited on-chain, this capital will inevitably seek productive uses.This dynamic is very similar to the rise of “private lending” over the past decade: due to regulatory pressure, traditional banks withdrew from certain lending sectors, while new capital structures filled the gaps. The 2.0 version of on-chain lending will depart from the speculative nature of early DeFi. It will no longer be a simple matter of "collateralizing Ethereum in one hand and lending stablecoins in the other" for speculation, but will instead shift towards a productive credit economy—providing liquidity for real assets, accounts receivable, and emerging market businesses forgotten by the traditional banking system. This transformation even has geopolitical significance. Through the GENIUS Act, the US government is essentially betting that every cryptocurrency wallet represents a new node in the dollar system. This direct, intermediary-free dollar distribution channel is extending the dollar's influence to corners never reached by the Bretton Woods system. Conclusion We are witnessing a complete upgrade of the financial system—the underlying infrastructure has been replaced with an open, programmable, and natively interoperable blockchain. Stablecoins not only solve payment problems, but also address issues of idle capital, insufficient credit, and excessively high investment thresholds. The payment layer is where accounts are opened, while the credit and investment layers are where wealth truly grows. The companies that position themselves within these layers of the economic stack today will define the next generation of the global dollar economy.

data.title: DeepSeek receives a major update and finally "opens its eyes"!
data.description:
Just when we thought this crazy AI update frenzy was over, DeepSeek, which just released V4 last week, suddenly dropped an even bigger surprise.

Just now, DeepSeek launched its image recognition mode, currently in beta testing. This means that DeepSeek's multimodal capabilities, which have been discussed for a whole year, are finally here!

Currently, both the DeepSeek web version and app may be able to access the image recognition mode after updates. APPSO conducted a test for everyone as soon as possible.

Chen Xiaokang, a researcher at DeepSeek responsible for multimodal processing, posted on X: Now, we see you, along with an image. Let's have DeepSeek interpret what this image means.

The results show that it can recognize the metaphor behind the image. Although there are no words related to DeepSeek in the image, it combines the recognition of the publisher's identity and the image itself to infer that this is an update to DeepSeek's multimodal capabilities. Finally, a very apt summary: The whale that couldn't see the world has finally opened its eyes.

Compared to the answer result, APPSO found the thought process of DeepSeek's image recognition mode to be more interesting.

In the past, when AI looked at that tweet screenshot, it would most likely have honestly described it as: "Two blue whales, the one on the left is wearing an eye patch, the one on the right isn't."

But DeepSeek immediately started asking: Who is this person? Why did they post this? What does the whale logo represent? What does the XX on the eye patch imply?

This is what really happens in our minds when we come across a meme. Nobody counts how many whales there are; we care about who's talking to whom and what the subtext is. And it even self-corrects itself. For example, it even briefly compared the eye patch in the picture to Kamina's glasses in *Gurren Lagann*, then contradicted itself: "No, that's too otaku-ish." "Wait, look closely..." "Let's try another angle..." The preceding reasoning, associations, and self-corrections were quite brilliant. But the most counterintuitive part of the entire thought process was actually when it was almost finished reasoning, it suddenly paused itself and held a mini-defense session.

It lists three questions and answers itself,first confirming the objective facts, then speculating on the nature of the event, and finally making an interpretation. DeepSeek has turned this thinking habit, which we ourselves are not even aware of, into the logic of image recognition. Just like how we usually think through things before drawing conclusions: "Wait, is this premise correct? Does that assumption hold water? What if I've misunderstood the direction?" We also posed the classic AI test question—counting fingers—to DeepSeek. It thought for a while, but still answered incorrectly, even muttering, "I'm really dizzy from counting." However, if I guide it a little further, it can still answer correctly.

In another finger-counting test, after the first incorrect answer, I didn't give it an answer, but just let it think again, and it still gave the correct answer.

data.title: The KelpDAO $292 Million Scandal Reveals the Real Blind Spots in Blockchain Security
data.description:
Author: Merkle3s Capital Source: X, @Merkle3sCapital

$292 million, not a code problem

On April 18, 2026, another huge loss was added to the history of DeFi—the attackers did not discover any smart contract vulnerabilities, did not use any zero-day vulnerabilities, and did not even need any advanced technology. They simply exploited a configuration option: KelpDAO set the security verification of the cross-chain bridge to 1-of-1.

Here's what happened. KelpDAO used LayerZero as the cross-chain bridge protocol to transfer rsETH (Restaked ETH) between Ethereum and various Layer 2 blockchains.

The core security of cross-chain bridges is a verification mechanism called DVN (Decentralized Verifier Network)—simply put, it determines how many independent verifier nodes are needed to confirm "this cross-chain transaction is genuine" before the message is allowed to pass. KelpDAO's configuration is 1-of-1: one node's confirmation is sufficient. Attackers (according to security companies like SlowMist, pointing to North Korea's Lazarus Group) interfered with normal RPC nodes through a DDoS attack while simultaneously injecting forged data into a single DVN. The single verifier node was fooled, the fake cross-chain message was approved—116,500 rsETH (approximately $292 million) were thus minted, and the non-existent other end of the Ethereum network "crossed" over. The attackers then deposited the forged rsETH into Aave V3 as collateral, borrowing approximately $190 million worth of WETH. The entire process involved no cryptographic cracking and no exploitation of code vulnerabilities. The consequences were cascading. According to an incident report released by the Aave governance forum on April 20, the bad debt amounted to approximately $230 million (Scenario 2 estimate). According to CoinDesk, Aave's TVL plummeted from approximately $48.5 billion to approximately $30-31 billion within days, a drop of over 37%. According to Yahoo Finance, the AAVE token has fallen by about 15-20% (it peaked at about 20%). Lending rates surged from 3.4% to 14%, and the total TVL of the entire DeFi market evaporated by over $13 billion in just a few days. This is a disaster where AI can perfectly audit code, but it can never audit configuration parameters. AI auditing tools are exploding—and then what? If you follow the blockchain security circle, you've seen an exciting narrative over the past two years: AI is finally going to solve the security problems of DeFi. This isn't just empty talk. CertiK's Skynet platform covers real-time on-chain monitoring of thousands of projects; De.Fi Scanner has accumulated over a million scans; Hexagate's on-chain threat detection has been acquired by Gate.io. The academic community is also following suit—several papers on smart contract vulnerability detection based on large language models were published between 2025 and 2026, reporting impressive detection rates. AI is indeed effective at the code level. Reentrancy attacks, integer overflows, access control flaws—AI finds these known patterns much faster than humans. Industry surveys show that 60-80% of audit projects are already using AI tools as an initial screening aid, without exception among leading audit firms. But a paradox lies here: as tools improve, losses do not decrease.

DeFiLlama's data doesn't lie. Since 2025, there have been more than 15 major attacks resulting in losses exceeding $30 million, with total losses exceeding $2 billion.

What role did AI auditing tools play in these incidents? The answer is: almost none. Because the root causes of these attacks are almost never at the code level.

KelpDAO was audited, and then what?

KelpDAO is not some obscure little project—it underwent a smart contract audit by a security firm, and its underlying protocol, LayerZero, is one of the most widely used cross-chain infrastructures in the industry.

The audit report is in black and white, the code is clean. And then it lost $292 million.

Here's a key distinction: The audit covered the smart contract code, but not the DVN configuration parameters. The audit firm checked for vulnerabilities in the contract logic—which it didn't. However, the audit didn't cover the risks associated with your 1-of-1 DVN configuration in a production environment. Ironically, after the incident, a public dispute arose between KelpDAO and LayerZero regarding liability. According to CoinDesk, KelpDAO claimed that the 1-of-1 configuration is the default setting for LayerZero, with its quickstart guide and default GitHub configuration pointing to 1/1 DVN, and approximately 40% of the LayerZero protocol using this configuration. LayerZero countered that they had explicitly warned against using 1-of-1 configurations in production. After the incident, LayerZero completely banned 1-of-1 configurations—but this was a case of locking the barn door after the horse had bolted. Regardless of who is right or wrong—this controversy itself illustrates one thing: The key to security decisions lies not in the code, but in human communication, documentation, default values, and what we assume you know. AI auditing tools can scan every function call in the code, but they won't ask, "How many verification nodes does your cross-chain bridge use?" This isn't because AI isn't smart enough; it's because it simply doesn't know to ask that question. The Four Blind Spots of AI Auditing Security researcher samczsun (former Paradigm security researcher and founder of SEAL 911) essentially said: The difference between those who find vulnerabilities and those who don't is that the former can observe inconsistencies and persistently investigate. Blind Spot One: Architectural Design Decisions AI can audit code logic, but it cannot determine whether the security assumptions of the system are valid. KelpDAO is a typical example: a 1-of-1 DVN is completely legal at the code level—the contract logic has no bugs, function calls are correct, and access control is reasonable. However, architecturally, it creates a single point of failure. AI sees "correct code," but not "design flaws." Similar cases are not uncommon. Multichain's MPC keys are controlled by the CEO alone, and Beanstalk Farms' governance proposals lack time locks—the code is fine, but the architecture is a ticking time bomb. Blind Spot Two: Configuration Parameter Auditing When smart contracts are deployed on-chain, numerous parameters need to be set—the number of verification nodes, multi-signature threshold, time lock duration, oracle source, and liquidation threshold. These parameters determine the actual security level of the system, but they are not in the code, but in the deployment configuration. AI auditing tools scan the logic of .sol files, not the parameters you actually fill in during deployment. It's like an auditor checking that the lock design of a bank vault is sound, but no one checks whether the door is actually locked during operation. KelpDAO's DVN = 1 means "the door isn't locked." The Nomad Bridge disaster in 2022 was even more outrageous—during a code upgrade after the audit, the Merkle root was initialized to 0x00, meaning any message could be forged as valid. This isn't audit negligence; it's audit drift: the audited code and the final deployed code are not the same. Blind Spot Three: Operational Processes and Private Key Management AI cannot see the configuration of the Hardware Security Module (HSM), where the private keys are stored, the operator's OpSec level, or whether temporary permissions have been revoked. The Ronin Network case is the most painful: attackers obtained the private keys of five validator nodes (four belonging to Sky Mavis and one to a third-party Axie DAO validator), and a temporary authorization in 2021 was never revoked. The attackers stole $624 million. The attack was not discovered until six days later—because no one was monitoring the abnormal behavior of the validator nodes. Mixin Network stores its private keys in a cloud database. Bybit's Safe multi-signature frontend was injected with malicious JavaScript by Lazarus. The interface seen by the signer was completely different from the actual transaction being signed—the contract itself remained intact, rendering AI contract audits completely ineffective. Blind Spot Four: Combined Risks DeFi is like Lego bricks. The security of a single module does not equate to security when combined. KelpDAO's architecture consists of three layers: rsETH (re-staking protocol) + LayerZero (cross-chain bridge) + Aave V3 (lending protocol). Each layer is reasonable on its own—but the risk of combining them is exponential. "What happens to Aave's collateral if the cross-chain bridge is compromised?"—This question is not included in any audit report because it is not within the scope of auditing any single module. OpenZeppelin, in its post-KelpDAO analysis, explicitly points out: "Smart contract audits typically don't check how third-party protocol integrations are configured during deployment, nor do they check whether infrastructure components introduce single points of failure." AI currently cannot do this—it audits the code, not the intent. Case Study: This isn't an isolated incident; it's a pattern. If you think KelpDAO was just an unfortunate accident, the following three cases will make you rethink. Case Study 1: Bybit—$1.46 billion, malicious code injected into the front end. Time: 2025 Losses on March 21: Approximately $1.46 billion (401,347 ETH + stETH and other tokens) Attack Type: Supply Chain Attack Bybit uses Safe as its multi-signature wallet solution. An attacker (identified by the FBI as Lazarus Group) compromised a Safe developer's machine, injecting malicious code into Safe's front-end JavaScript via an AWS S3 bucket. When Bybit's multi-signature signers opened the Safe interface to sign, they saw normal transaction content—but the actual signed transaction had been tampered with, transferring control to the attacker. Key Points: The smart contract itself had no vulnerabilities. The multi-signature logic was correct, and the code was audited. The attack occurred at the front end—a level that an AI contract auditing tool would not even check. This is the largest single loss in DeFi history. Case Study 2: Ronin Network – $624 Million, Temporary Permissions Forgot to Revoke

Date: March 23, 2022 (Disclosed March 29) Loss: Approximately $624 million (173,600 ETH + 25.5M USDC) Attack Type: Private Key Leak + Operational Security Failure

Ronin is a sidechain of Axie Infinity, where withdrawals can be approved by 5 out of 9 validator nodes. Attackers used social engineering to compromise the devices of Sky Mavis employees, obtaining the private keys of 5 validator nodes (4 belonging to Sky Mavis and 1 to a third-party Axie DAO validator). The permissions for some of these keys came from a temporary authorization in 2021—which should have been revoked after the task was completed, but no one remembered to do so.

data.title: The FBI's appearance at the "Bitcoin 2026" conference: reconciliation or assimilation?
data.description:
Author: Liam 'Akiba' Wright   Translator: Saoirse, Foresight News

On April 27, 2026, the "Bitcoin 2026" conference officially opened at the Venetian Hotel. The FBI Director was invited to attend and participate in a forum on code, free speech, and regulatory enforcement. This arrangement made this industry event a real-world test of Bitcoin's political nature.

The forum, titled "Code Is Speech: The Battle to End Bitcoin Regulation," was held at 10:30 AM on the Satoshi Nakamoto Stage, moderated by Paul Grewal, with Acting U.S. Attorney General Todd Blanche participating in person. Patel did not attend in person but participated in the roundtable discussion online.

Todd Blanche is the current Acting Attorney General of the United States and also serves as the 40th Deputy Attorney General. The symbolic significance of this lineup is self-evident: the "Bitcoin 2026" conference brings together law enforcement officials, senior officials from the U.S. Department of Justice, regulatory officials, politicians, corporate finance executives, and Wall Street digital asset industry leaders into a single discourse arena. And the source of all this is a decentralized movement centered on peer-to-peer settlement and user-managed custody. Over the years, Bitcoin has gradually and deeply integrated into various institutional systems, and outsiders easily attribute internal opposition to simply emotional venting on social media. But at a deeper level, a core issue concerning the essence of the industry is now before everyone. Today, Bitcoin has achieved the mainstream legitimacy that early adopters dreamed of: gaining policy attention, listing on listed companies' balance sheets, launching compliant ETFs, and being included in the U.S. National Strategic Reserve Asset Plan. However, the cost is equally significant: the mainstream discourse power regarding Bitcoin's popularization and development is now firmly in the hands of various traditional institutions—while Bitcoin's original purpose was to break free from dependence on these intermediaries. A Policy Victory Reshaping the Industry Landscape The core highlight of this conference lies in the major shift in regulatory enforcement logic. In a Department of Justice memorandum released in April 2025, Blanche explicitly stated that the U.S. Department of Justice is not a dedicated regulatory body for digital assets and prohibits prosecutors from using criminal prosecutions to indirectly regulate the industry. The document also requires that the focus of investigations into digital asset-related cases should be on protecting investors and combating cryptocurrency-related criminal activities. This memorandum formally disbanded the U.S. Cryptocurrency Enforcement Task Force. This new regulatory tone lays the foundation for the core theme of this year's conference: "Embracing Developers, Embracing Technological Innovation." Blanche and Patel, at the "Bitcoin 2026" conference, released a clear signal: the target of regulation is crypto-criminal activity, not developers and underlying code technology. This shift in regulatory direction was foreshadowed. CryptoSlate previously reported that the U.S. government is comprehensively relaxing enforcement controls in the cryptocurrency field, with the disbanding of the national cryptocurrency enforcement agency being a key measure. The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission has also adjusted its enforcement strategy, overturning past crypto regulatory enforcement guidelines and rejecting its previous high-pressure regulatory approach. In short, the core stance of the US government is very clear: developers should create neutral blockchain tools without incurring excessive legal risks; the use of digital assets for fraud, money laundering, and other illegal activities will remain a key target for law enforcement. This statement directly addresses the core objective of Bitcoin's inception. The Bitcoin white paper envisions a peer-to-peer electronic cash system, allowing transacting parties to bypass traditional financial institutions and conduct direct transactions. The crypto community, rooted in this concept, remains wary of traditional intermediaries re-infiltrating the system, and this forum on code and freedom of speech directly addresses this core contradiction from a legal perspective. In April 2026, Coin Center's open letter to the US SEC defined the legal boundaries for freedom of speech protection for open-source software and neutral tools; simultaneously, asset custody, centralized control, and targeted commercial operations were categorized under regulatory compliance. This has become the core standpoint for US regulators: if federal agencies can reduce the legal risks for developers and stop lumping together technology creators with illegal users, the Bitcoin industry can gain more lenient development space in the US. However, a contradiction arises: if this lenient policy environment ultimately comes from a national power system that many native crypto users distrust, then this victory will inevitably come at the cost of compromising industry principles. This year's Bitcoin conference laid bare this contradiction and division before the public. Even if regulations become more tolerant of developers, when official power stands at the core of the Bitcoin stage, this reconciliation is more like a state-led exchange of interests, and controversy and disagreement are inevitable. Institutionalization has become the mainstream path for the popularization of cryptocurrencies. In 2025, the White House signed the "Strategic Bitcoin Reserve Executive Order," formally establishing the US strategic Bitcoin reserve plan and digital asset reserve mechanism. As of press time, CryptoSlate market data shows that the price of Bitcoin is approximately $76,258, with a total market capitalization approaching $1.53 trillion. Participating in crypto asset investment through compliant channels has become a mainstream trend in the industry. As of April 27, the net asset value of the BlackRock iShares Bitcoin Trust ETF was approximately $62.34 billion, and the assets under custody of Coinbase's institutional business exceeded $300 billion. At the corporate level, Strategy& announced on April 27th that it had added another 3,273 Bitcoins to its holdings, bringing its total Bitcoin holdings to 818,334. Bitcoin has now deeply penetrated the reserve assets of listed companies, compliant ETF products, institutional custody platforms, and national strategic policies. An industry event aimed at widespread adoption will naturally attract institutional players with industry channels and resources. Government, law enforcement, compliant ETFs, custodians, and corporate reserve assets each represent the industry's progress and achievements, but also harbor the hidden danger of diluting the decentralized concept: The US designation of Bitcoin as a strategic reserve asset grants official industry recognition, but also weakens the core consensus of user autonomy; The Department of Justice relaxes developer regulations, creating a friendly innovation environment, but allows law enforcement to deeply intervene in the core of crypto culture; Compliant ETFs provide low-barrier participation channels for ordinary investors, but gradually marginalize the original concepts of independent private key holding and peer-to-peer trading; Leading custodians provide infrastructure for large institutions, but concentrate asset control in the hands of centralized compliant intermediaries; The large-scale holdings of Bitcoin by corporations have reinforced the Bitcoin consensus, gradually giving capital giants more influence than ordinary individual users. While institutional implementation has solved practical problems for industry development, it has also led Bitcoin back into the centralized dependence it once sought to escape. This is the core source of community controversy: institutions see it as progress in industry standardization; decentralized believers see it as a deviation from its original purpose. From a practical perspective, the increased adoption also means fewer users are choosing to hold their own private keys or use on-chain direct settlement; the public is increasingly reliant on regulated third-party platforms. This image is a data dashboard released at the "Bitcoin 2026" conference, visually showcasing the current mainstreaming and institutionalization of Bitcoin, and the underlying contradiction of "victory" and "co-optation." The battle for discourse power: Who has the right to define Bitcoin? This year's conference featured an unprecedentedly diverse lineup of speakers, including regulatory officials, US politicians, Wall Street asset management giants, corporate reserve asset operators, and native crypto practitioners. This diverse lineup can be interpreted as strong proof that Bitcoin has completely won mainstream acceptance, but it also signifies that Bitcoin's public narrative and industry culture are being repackaged and redefined by institutions with completely different interests. The protocol itself remains open and free, but public opinion and value narratives surrounding Bitcoin have become centralized. Two types of viewpoints on overseas social media platforms bluntly express the community's concerns. Veteran crypto users publicly criticized the conference for inviting federal law enforcement officials; crypto fraud investigators stated that Bitcoin is gradually becoming the old system it initially wanted to escape. Corporate financial statements holding positions, strong regulation, political endorsement, centralized custody, mining giant monopolies… all these signs are seen as signals that the industry is deviating from its original purpose. Many community users echoed this sentiment; the core of this controversy is not the attendance of a single guest, but the allocation of power. When the core industry stage for Bitcoin is occupied by government officials, financial institutions, corporate capital, and politicians, the public's perception of Bitcoin will ultimately contradict its original slogan of "self-custody and decentralization." The 2024 controversy surrounding Michael Saylor's self-custody agreement already proved that fierce battles for power will always erupt in the industry when there are disagreements about Bitcoin's development path. However, from a practical perspective, large-scale adoption inevitably requires legal compliance, asset custody, open financial markets, and policy coordination.
