Bitcoin is now a super asset with a market capitalization of $1.5 trillion, and can no longer be confined to a niche decentralized community. The core issue for the future is never about completely rejecting institutions, but rather how to prevent institutional channels from completely monopolizing the definition of Bitcoin. The power struggle will become the next ultimate test. Since BlackRock submitted its Bitcoin ETF application in 2024, and subsequently Bitcoin was included in the US election strategy, a division in Bitcoin's identity has been quietly brewing, and the "Bitcoin 2026" conference completely exposed this rift. Two opposing realities objectively exist simultaneously: government intervention has reduced industry policy uncertainty, ETFs and custodian institutions have broadened the barriers to entry, and corporate reserve assets have propelled Bitcoin to become a mainstream global reserve asset—these are undeniable development dividends. However, the accompanying hidden dangers cannot be ignored: compliant products weaken users' autonomy over their assets, large capital and enterprises dominate industry discourse, and political forces reshape the crypto narrative. Bitcoin, once labeled as "free from authoritative control," now has to actively embrace law enforcement and integrate into the mainstream order. After the conference, the real test lies in control. Can the industry uphold its core principles: insist on user-owned custody, open-source ecosystem development, and peer-to-peer direct settlement, making institutional development merely supplementary rather than dominant? Or will it allow popularization channels to become entirely dependent on ETFs, centralized custody, and cooperation between corporate capital and policy, relegating decentralization to a marginal concept? The core of "The Bitcoin Control Test" is to reveal the fundamental tension between the "user-native route" and the "institutional mainstream route" in the development of Bitcoin. On one hand, there is the convenient and compliant popularization; on the other hand, there is the native freedom and decentralized belief. This tug-of-war between the two routes makes Bitcoin's victory inherently contradictory. Those traditional institutions that were once vehemently avoided by Bitcoin users are now becoming the driving force and advocates for the industry's mainstream adoption. Some see this as the ultimate victory for the industry's maturity, while others see it as a warning sign for the decentralization ideal. The "Bitcoin 2026" conference clearly demonstrated that, regardless of one's stance, everyone must confront this irreversible era of change.

data.title: Kong vs. Godzilla: Why is Musk suing Ultraman?
data.description:
Tao Zhu, Jinse Finance

Summary

On April 27, the case of Musk v. Altman officially went to trial. Musk sued OpenAI, CEO Altman, President Greg Brockman, and Microsoft, claiming they betrayed him and the public, abandoned OpenAI's mission as a philanthropic administrator of artificial intelligence for the benefit of humanity, and transformed the non-profit organization into a for-profit giant. Altman, on the other hand, pointed out that Musk's lawsuit was not really about whether OpenAI was philanthropic, but rather because xAI was inferior to OpenAI.

I. A War of Words Triggered by "Non-profit"

The trial of OpenAI's two founders, Altman and Musk, was held in California, with their arguments clearly differing. Musk believed that Altman changed OpenAI's philanthropic nature; Altman, in turn, believed that Musk intended to suppress OpenAI.

(A punching bag with images of Musk and Ultraman appeared outside the courthouse.)

1. Musk: Stealing money from charities is wrong

At the court hearing, Musk wore a dark suit and tie.

During his testimony, he stated: "The trial is actually very simple: stealing money from charities is wrong… If plundering charities is acceptable, then the entire foundation of charitable giving is destroyed." With the rapid development of artificial intelligence technology, Musk has become increasingly concerned about the field and worried that "the government has not taken effective measures" to regulate it. Musk's lawyer, Steven Molo, noted that Musk's concerns intensified after a meeting with then-President Obama in 2015. He said his client has always believed that artificial intelligence "is not a tool for people to get rich." When OpenAI was still a non-profit organization, Musk donated $38 million to it over several years. "Without Elon Musk, there would be no OpenAI. It's that simple." When Musk's lawyer asked him if he would still donate money, time, and effort to OpenAI if its mission were not to be a non-profit organization developing technology to benefit humanity, Musk replied that he would not. "What I mean is, the idea, the name, I came up with it, I recruited the key people, taught them everything I knew, provided all the seed funding—otherwise, I did nothing. It was a huge undertaking, and I deliberately chose to make it something that would benefit all of humanity." OpenAI had proposed opening a for-profit division in 2018, years before its launch of ChatGPT, which would open the commercial AI market. Musk is demanding billions of dollars in "illicit gains," which his lawyers claim will be used to fund OpenAI's non-profit division. He also wants to radically reform the company, including firing Altman. 2. OpenAI: Musk Attempts to Suppress "Competitors" An OpenAI lawyer stated that the motivation for the lawsuit is Musk's attempt to suppress "competitors." William Savitt, the lawyer, stated, “We are here because Mr. Musk’s plans for OpenAI have not succeeded. Because he is our competitor, Mr. Musk will attack OpenAI by any means necessary.” OpenAI’s lawyers stated that Musk used his investments to “bully” other OpenAI founders, and that he also wanted to merge the company with his Tesla. “The other founders refused to hand over control of artificial intelligence to one person. When they refused to allow OpenAI to be acquired, Musk took his head home.” Unable to control OpenAI, he abandoned it. He believed at the time that OpenAI was finished. “Musk never cared whether OpenAI was a non-profit organization.” OpenAI described the dispute as a battle for control, not a fight over establishing a for-profit division: Musk wanted control of the for-profit company, but “we could not agree on for-profit terms with Elon because we believed that giving any individual absolute control over OpenAI would violate our mission.” "We regret that we've come to this point with someone we deeply admire—someone who inspired us to pursue higher goals, then told us we would fail, started a competing company, and then sued us just as we were beginning to make meaningful progress toward OpenAI's mission without him." OpenAI states that Musk's move stems from jealousy and regret over leaving the company. As the race for Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) intensifies, OpenAI accuses Musk of hindering the development of one of its main competitors. Musk's own platform, xAI, developed the chatbot Grok but has consistently lagged behind its competitors. xAI debuted in 2023, a year after ChatGPT. It is believed that Musk understands the company's decision to open a for-profit division, and that he left the company simply because he failed to become its CEO. Altman is also expected to testify during the trial. The verdict is expected to be announced in late May. II. From Allies to Rivals: The Past Between Musk and Altman This lawsuit concerns the future of artificial intelligence. Although only one person will likely win in the end, both could suffer significant losses in this ordeal. This battle has been likened to two heavyweight boxers entering the ring. One observer even compared it to King Kong versus Godzilla. But when they first met, Altman called Musk his idol. Musk and Altman met in 2012 through a Silicon Valley investor. Altman, then in his early twenties and 14 years younger than Musk, later introduced Musk to the concept of OpenAI. They both believed in the potential of this technology. In 2015 At a joint meeting in 2015, Musk stated that artificial intelligence is the technology "most likely to change humanity," but he also believed that AI is "very unstable" and "fraught with difficulties." (Musk and Altman pictured together in 2015, the year they co-founded OpenAI.) OpenAI was officially established in December 2015. Positioned as a non-profit organization, it aimed to safely develop AI, benefit all of humanity, and prevent AI from being monopolized by giants. In 2017, with the surge in demand for AI computing power, Musk advocated for its rapid commercialization. OpenAI was merged into Tesla, but Altman insisted on prioritizing non-profit and cautious commercialization, leading to public internal conflicts. In February 2018, these conflicts escalated, and Musk resigned as co-chairman and withdrew from the board, retaining only a small number of shares. His public reasons were managing Tesla, insufficient energy for X, and avoiding conflicts of interest. Privately, he believed OpenAI was "too academic, too slow, and doomed to fail." Following this incident, Altman took over as CEO, leading the subsequent commercialization strategy. In March 2019, OpenAI was officially restructured. In July, it received a $1 billion investment from Microsoft and an exclusive partnership with Azure cloud computing power. OpenAI began to close its core models (such as GPT-2, which is not fully open source). This drew criticism from Musk, who claimed that OpenAI had been controlled by Microsoft, betrayed its non-profit principles, and become a "closed-source for-profit company." In November 2022, ChatGPT (GPT-3.5) was launched, gaining over 100 million users in 5 days and becoming a global sensation. Valuation soared. In May 2023, Microsoft invested an additional $10 billion, acquiring approximately 49% of the shares (without control). In November, the OpenAI board wanted to fire Altman, but Microsoft strongly supported him. At the end of the month, Altman returned as CEO, restructured the board, and solidified his control. Musk was critical, calling OpenAI "completely commercialized and abandoning its non-profit commitment," and accelerating the development of its competitor, xAI. In 2024, GPT-4o was released, leading in multimodal capabilities; OpenAI's valuation reached $800 billion. During the same period, xAI's valuation was about half that of OpenAI. In May of that year, xAI completed a $6 billion Series B funding round, with a post-money valuation of $240 billion; in December, it completed a $6 billion Series C funding round, with a valuation of $400-500 billion. In March of that year, xAI released its first large model, Grok-1, for testing within a small X-ray range; in August, it released Grok-2. The preview version has a context window size of 256k, approaching the level of GPT-4, but its overall capabilities are still slightly weaker than GPT-4. In December 2025, Musk officially sued OpenAI and Altman, claiming that OpenAI's 2019 restructuring was illegal, demanding a return to a purely non-profit organization, the removal of Altman, and the return of control; the lawsuit continues to this day and is still escalating. III. Is the lawsuit based on unfair competition? Looking at the longer timeline, Musk's desire to acquire OpenAI is not new. As mentioned above, in 2017, Musk proposed merging OpenAI into Tesla, escalating the conflict with Altman. On February 14, 2025, Musk, leading a consortium of investment institutions, formally submitted an acquisition offer to OpenAI. Specifically, Musk, as the lead investor, along with Valor Equity Partners, Baron Capital, Atreides Management, and other investment institutions, proposed to acquire OpenAI for $97.4 billion. The non-profit parent company. Musk publicly stated that the acquisition was to allow OpenAI to "return to the forces of open source and security," reverse the commercialization path led by Altman, restore its original purely non-profit mission from its founding in 2015, and prevent OpenAI from being further controlled by capital and deviating from its public welfare mission. Meanwhile, his lawyers added that if OpenAI agreed to stop its transformation into a for-profit company, Musk would withdraw the acquisition offer. On the same day, the OpenAI board formally rejected the acquisition proposal, clearly stating that "OpenAI is not for sale." The board unanimously believed that the acquisition proposal was not in the company's best interests and was a strategy by Musk to "disrupt competition and slow down OpenAI's development." Altman also claimed that the acquisition offer was a tactic of interference from competitors, emphasizing that OpenAI's core mission is to create AGI that benefits humanity, and it would not accept any acquisition arrangements that deviate from this mission. Since then, Musk has further strengthened his control over OpenAI. The lawsuit's stance also lends suspicion of "unfair competition." Altman once told Musk, "I'm very grateful for everything you've done to help—I don't think there would be OpenAI without you—and it really hurts when you publicly attack OpenAI." Musk responded, "I heard you. I certainly didn't intend to hurt you, and I deeply apologize for that, but the fate of civilization is in jeopardy." For both Musk and OpenAI, this matter is extremely significant. Until the end of 2023, Musk was still advocating for a halt to AI development. But now, Musk is deeply involved in the race for Artificial General Intelligence (AGI). Ross Chan-Louis, executive director and law professor at the Lowell Milken Center for Philanthropy and Nonprofits at UCLA, points out, "If Musk wins, it could lead to the defeat of a major competitor in the AGI race. Whoever wins this race wields considerable power."
